```{r min-primary-paternity-models, echo=FALSE, results='hide', fig.show='hide'}
#This models assumes minimum possible paternity for primary males

#Full sample (all group types)
if (file.exists("../output/model-objects/intercept_only_model_min.rds")) {
  intercept_only_model_min <- readRDS("../output/model-objects/intercept_only_model_min.rds")
} else {
  intercept_only_model_min <- brm(
    bf(proportion_primary_minimum_adj | weights(n_paternities^0.5) ~
         (1 | gr(genus_species_subspecies, cov = phylo_matrix)) +
         (1 | species_nonphylo)),
    family = Beta(),
    data = data_clean,
    data2 = list(phylo_matrix = phylo_matrix),
    prior = c(
      prior(normal(0, 1), class = "Intercept"),
      prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "sd"),
      prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "phi")
    ),
    chains = 4, iter = 10000, seed = 123,
    control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99)
  )
  saveRDS(intercept_only_model_min, "../output/model-objects/intercept_only_model_min.rds")
}

  summary(intercept_only_model_min)
  plot(intercept_only_model_min)
  pp_check(intercept_only_model_min, type="hist")
  pp_check(intercept_only_model_min, type="dens_overlay")
  
  n_obs_iom_min <- nobs(intercept_only_model_min)
  n_species_iom_min <- length(unique(
    sub("^([^_]+_[^_]+).*", "\\1", intercept_only_model$data$genus_species_subspecies)
  ))
  n_pats_iom_min <- sum(intercept_only_model_min$data$n_paternities, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  # Determine R2 
  bayes_R2_iom_min <- as.data.frame(bayes_R2(intercept_only_model_min))
  R2_est_iom_min <- round(bayes_R2_iom_min$Estimate[1], 2)
  R2_ci_lo_iom_min <- round(bayes_R2_iom_min$Q2.5[1], 2)
  R2_ci_hi_iom_min <- round(bayes_R2_iom_min$Q97.5[1], 2)

  # Extract posterior draws
  post_iom_min <- as_draws_df(intercept_only_model_min)
  
  # Extract and square standard deviations to get variances
  var_phylo_iom_min <- post_iom_min$`sd_genus_species_subspecies__Intercept`^2
  var_nonphylo_iom_min <- post_iom_min$`sd_species_nonphylo__Intercept`^2
  
  # Calculate lambda
  lambda_iom_min <- var_phylo_iom_min / (var_phylo_iom_min + var_nonphylo_iom_min)
  
  # Summarize lambda
  summary_lambda_iom_min <- quantile(lambda_iom_min, probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))
  summary_lambda_iom_min

```
Species-level random effects, which includes both phylogenetic relatedness and other species-level differences, explained roughly 35-40% of the variation in the proportion of paternities obtained by primary males. The posterior mean $R^2$ values of intercept-only models for all group types when all uncertain paternities were assigned to the primary male (`r format(R2_est_iom, nsmall=2)`, 95% CI: `r format(R2_ci_lo_iom, nsmall=2)` - `r format(R2_ci_hi_iom, nsmall=2)`, n=`r n_obs_iom` observations, `r analysis_species` species, and `r format(n_pats_iom, big.mark = ",")` total paternities) was very similar to the $R^2$ value when none of the uncertain paternities were assigned to the primary male (`r format(R2_est_iom_min, nsmall=2)`, 95% CI: `r format(R2_ci_lo_iom_min, nsmall=2)` - `r format(R2_ci_hi_iom_min, nsmall=2)`).  

We next examined how this explained variation was apportioned between phylogenetic relatedness and other, non-phylogenetic species-level factors. If we assume that primary males sired all infants of uncertain paternity, a substantial proportion of the variation in primary male paternity share was explained by phylogenetic relatedness in the full sample ($\lambda$=`r format(round(summary_lambda_iom[2], 2), nsmall = 2)`, 95% CI: `r format(round(summary_lambda_iom[1], 2), nsmall = 2)` - `r format(round(summary_lambda_iom[3], 2), nsmall = 2)`). Assigning all uncertain paternities to other, non-primary males decreased $\lambda$ and increased 95% CIs ($\lambda$=`r format(round(summary_lambda_iom_min[2], 2), nsmall = 2)`, 95% CI: `r format(round(summary_lambda_iom_min[1], 2), nsmall = 2)` - `r format(round(summary_lambda_iom_min[3], 2), nsmall = 2)`). 

**Question 2: How do group composition and reproductive seasonality influence primary males' paternity share?**

```{r summary-stats-paternity-loss, echo=FALSE}

#Calculate mean, SD aggregated across group types

mean_val_primary <- mean(data_clean$proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_val_primary <- sd(data_clean$proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE)
n_val_primary <- sum(!is.na(data_clean$proportion_primary_maximum_adj))
n_pats_primary <- sum(data_clean$n_paternities[!is.na(data_clean$proportion_primary_maximum_adj)], na.rm = TRUE)
n_species_primary <- n_distinct(data_clean$genus_species_subspecies[!is.na(data_clean$proportion_primary_maximum_adj)])

mean_val_primary_min <- mean(data_clean$proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_val_primary_min <- sd(data_clean$proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE)
n_val_primary_min <- sum(!is.na(data_clean$proportion_primary_minimum_adj))
n_pats_primary_min <- sum(data_clean$n_paternities[!is.na(data_clean$proportion_primary_minimum_adj)], na.rm = TRUE)
n_species_primary_min <- n_distinct(data_clean$genus_species_subspecies[!is.na(data_clean$proportion_primary_minimum_adj)])

# Formatted versions for inline text
mean_val_primary_s <- sprintf("%.0f", mean_val_primary * 100)
sd_val_primary_s <- sprintf("%.0f", sd_val_primary * 100)
mean_val_primary_min_s <- sprintf("%.0f", mean_val_primary_min * 100)
sd_val_primary_min_s <- sprintf("%.0f", sd_val_primary_min * 100)

```


```{r table-paternity-loss, echo=FALSE, results='hide'}

#This puts the summary-stats-paternity-loss info into table format. Not going to display until further below.

# keep the NA filters identical everywhere
valid_rows <- function(df) {
  !is.na(df$proportion_primary_minimum_adj) & !is.na(df$proportion_primary_maximum_adj)
}

# 1) Main table by grp_composition 
main_tbl <- data_clean %>%
  group_by(grp_composition) %>%
  summarise(
    n         = sum(valid_rows(cur_data_all())),
    n_species = n_distinct(species_10ktrees[valid_rows(cur_data_all())]),
    n_pats    = sum(n_paternities[valid_rows(cur_data_all())], na.rm = TRUE),
    min       = min(proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    max       = max(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_min  = mean(proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_min    = sd(proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_max  = mean(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_max    = sd(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
  group_display = if_else(
    grp_composition == "MF", 
    "MF (All)", 
    as.character(grp_composition)  # visible label
  ),
  is_sub = FALSE
)

# 2) MF sub-rows by pairbond_type (only if MF exists)
mf_sub <- data_clean %>%
  filter(grp_composition == "MF") %>%
  mutate(pairbond_type = replace_na(pairbond_type, "Unknown")) %>%
  group_by(grp_composition, pairbond_type) %>%
  summarise(
    n         = sum(valid_rows(cur_data_all())),
    n_species = n_distinct(species_10ktrees[valid_rows(cur_data_all())]),
    n_pats    = sum(n_paternities[valid_rows(cur_data_all())], na.rm = TRUE),
    min       = min(proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    max       = max(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_min  = mean(proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_min    = sd(proportion_primary_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_max  = mean(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_max    = sd(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # visible label for sub-rows; indent is styled in gt
    group_display = paste0(pairbond_type),
    is_sub = TRUE
  )

# 3) Bind, then order so sub-rows appear directly under MF; keeps original order of grp_comp
order_vec <- main_tbl$grp_composition
# place MF subrows immediately after the MF row
combined <- bind_rows(
  main_tbl,
  mf_sub
) %>%
  mutate(
    grp_order = match(grp_composition, order_vec),
    # make sure MF subrows come right after MF (grp_order + small epsilon)
    sub_bump  = ifelse(is_sub & grp_composition == "MF", 0.5, 0),
    sort_key  = grp_order + sub_bump
  ) %>%
  arrange(sort_key, grp_composition, is_sub, group_display)

# 4) Build gt table (indent MF sub-rows)
# tbl_grptype number is pre-computed in setup chunk as tbl_grptype_num
summary_table <- combined %>%
  select(group_display, n, n_species, n_pats,
         min, max, mean_min, sd_min, mean_max, sd_max, is_sub) %>%
  gt(rowname_col = "group_display") %>%
  tab_stubhead(label = "Group composition") %>%
  tab_header(
    title = paste0("Table ", tbl_grptype_num, ". Proportion of paternities obtained by primary males, by group type")
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    n               = "N obs",
    n_species       = "N species",
    n_pats          = "Total paternities",
    min             = "Min",
    max             = "Max",
    mean_min        = "Mean (min est.)",
    sd_min          = "SD (min est.)",
    mean_max        = "Mean (max est.)",
    sd_max          = "SD (max est.)"
  ) %>%
  # format numeric columns
  fmt_number(
    columns  = c(min, max, mean_min, sd_min, mean_max, sd_max),
    decimals = 2
  ) %>%
  # left-align numeric columns
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(align = "left"),
    locations = cells_body(
      columns = c(n, n_species, n_pats, min, max, mean_min, sd_min, mean_max, sd_max)
    )
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title(groups = "title")
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(everything())
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_stub(rows = !is_sub)
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_stubhead()
  ) %>%
  # indent the stub for sub-rows
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(indent = px(18)),
    locations = cells_stub(rows = is_sub)
  ) %>%
  # de-emphasize the sub-rows a little
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "gray30"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = is_sub, columns = everything())
  ) %>%
  tab_options(
    table.border.top.style = "none",
    table.font.size        = px(13),
    table.align            = "left"
  ) %>%
  cols_hide(columns = is_sub)

```


```{r paternity-loss-by-grp-type-model, echo=FALSE, results='hide'} 

#This is basically a fancy ANOVA; only purpose is to determine whether there are meaningful differences in the paternity share of different group types. 

if (file.exists("../output/model-objects/paternityloss_grptype_model.rds")) {
  paternityloss_grptype_model <- readRDS("../output/model-objects/paternityloss_grptype_model.rds")
} else {
paternityloss_grptype_model <- brm(
  bf(proportion_primary_maximum_adj | weights(n_paternities^0.5) ~
       grp_composition_recode +
       (1 | gr(genus_species_subspecies, cov = phylo_matrix)) +
       (1 | species_nonphylo)),
  family = Beta(),
  data = data_clean,
  data2 = list(phylo_matrix = phylo_matrix),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"),
    prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "sd"),
    prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "phi")
  ),
  chains = 4, iter = 10000, seed = 123,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99)
)
  saveRDS(paternityloss_grptype_model, "../output/model-objects/paternityloss_grptype_model.rds")
}

summary(paternityloss_grptype_model)
#plot(paternityloss_grptype_model)

###Convert to proportion scale for reporting purposes###

# 1. sanity check: levels exist and MM is reference (so no b_*MM column)
stopifnot(all(c("MM","MF","MFF") %in% levels(data_clean$grp_composition_recode)))
dr <- as_draws_df(paternityloss_grptype_model)

# 2. linear predictors (logit) from fixed effects
eta_MM  <- dr$b_Intercept
eta_MF  <- eta_MM + dr$`b_grp_composition_recodeMF`
eta_MFF <- eta_MM + dr$`b_grp_composition_recodeMFF`

# 3. map to response scale (proportions)
mu_MM  <- plogis(eta_MM)
mu_MF  <- plogis(eta_MF)
mu_MFF <- plogis(eta_MFF)

# helper that returns a numeric named vector
summ_vec <- function(x) c(mean = mean(x), lo = unname(quantile(x, 0.025)),
                          hi = unname(quantile(x, 0.975)))

# group means (response scale)
g_MM  <- summ_vec(mu_MM)
g_MF  <- summ_vec(mu_MF)
g_MFF <- summ_vec(mu_MFF)

# pairwise differences (absolute, response scale) — choose direction explicitly
diff_MM_MF_prop  <- mu_MM  - mu_MF
diff_MM_MFF_prop <- mu_MM  - mu_MFF
diff_MF_MFF_prop <- mu_MF  - mu_MFF

d_MM_MF_prop  <- summ_vec(diff_MM_MF_prop)
d_MM_MFF_prop <- summ_vec(diff_MM_MFF_prop)
d_MF_MFF_prop <- summ_vec(diff_MF_MFF_prop)

# 4. hard sanity checks (these will error if something goes off)
# If MFF has higher mean than MM on the logit scale, this should be strictly < 0:
stopifnot(d_MM_MFF_prop["hi"] < 0)

# 5. create fresh names for inline (to avoid collisions with old vars)
MM_mean_prop     <- g_MM["mean"];    MM_lo_prop <- g_MM["lo"];    MM_hi_prop <- g_MM["hi"]
MF_mean_prop     <- g_MF["mean"];    MF_lo_prop <- g_MF["lo"];    MF_hi_prop <- g_MF["hi"]
MFF_mean_prop    <- g_MFF["mean"];   MFF_lo_prop <- g_MFF["lo"];  MFF_hi_prop <- g_MFF["hi"]

# Save Analysis 1 MM mean for use in Question 3 text (before it is overwritten by Analysis 2)
MM_mean_prop_A1 <- g_MM["mean"]
MM_lo_prop_A1   <- g_MM["lo"]
MM_hi_prop_A1   <- g_MM["hi"]
MM_mean_pct_A1_s <- sprintf("%.0f", MM_mean_prop_A1 * 100)
MM_lo_pct_A1_s   <- sprintf("%.0f", MM_lo_prop_A1 * 100)
MM_hi_pct_A1_s   <- sprintf("%.0f", MM_hi_prop_A1 * 100)

MM_MF_diff_prop  <- d_MM_MF_prop["mean"];  MM_MF_lo_prop  <- d_MM_MF_prop["lo"];  MM_MF_hi_prop  <- d_MM_MF_prop["hi"]
MM_MFF_diff_prop <- d_MM_MFF_prop["mean"]; MM_MFF_lo_prop <- d_MM_MFF_prop["lo"]; MM_MFF_hi_prop <- d_MM_MFF_prop["hi"]
MF_MFF_diff_prop <- d_MF_MFF_prop["mean"]; MF_MFF_lo_prop <- d_MF_MFF_prop["lo"]; MF_MFF_hi_prop <- d_MF_MFF_prop["hi"]

# 6. (optional) formatted strings with new names
fmt2 <- function(z) sprintf('%.2f', z)
MM_mean_prop_s     <- fmt2(MM_mean_prop);     MM_lo_prop_s  <- fmt2(MM_lo_prop);   MM_hi_prop_s  <- fmt2(MM_hi_prop)
MF_mean_prop_s     <- fmt2(MF_mean_prop);     MF_lo_prop_s  <- fmt2(MF_lo_prop);   MF_hi_prop_s  <- fmt2(MF_hi_prop)
MFF_mean_prop_s    <- fmt2(MFF_mean_prop);    MFF_lo_prop_s <- fmt2(MFF_lo_prop);  MFF_hi_prop_s <- fmt2(MFF_hi_prop)

MM_MF_diff_prop_s  <- fmt2(MM_MF_diff_prop);  MM_MF_lo_prop_s  <- fmt2(MM_MF_lo_prop);  MM_MF_hi_prop_s  <- fmt2(MM_MF_hi_prop)
MM_MFF_diff_prop_s <- fmt2(MM_MFF_diff_prop); MM_MFF_lo_prop_s <- fmt2(MM_MFF_lo_prop); MM_MFF_hi_prop_s <- fmt2(MM_MFF_hi_prop)
MF_MFF_diff_prop_s <- fmt2(MF_MFF_diff_prop); MF_MFF_lo_prop_s <- fmt2(MF_MFF_lo_prop); MF_MFF_hi_prop_s <- fmt2(MF_MFF_hi_prop)

# Count MMF and MMFF observations for inline reporting
n_mmf  <- sum(data_analysis$grp_composition == "MMF", na.rm = TRUE)
n_mmff <- sum(data_analysis$grp_composition == "MMFF", na.rm = TRUE)

```

```{r data-consistency-check-question-2, echo=FALSE, results='hide', eval=FALSE}

#Verify that the observations being used in the table and model are all the same

#1--Identify the rows the table uses
table_row_ids <- data_clean %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(proportion_primary_minimum_adj) &
    !is.na(proportion_primary_maximum_adj)
  ) %>%
  pull(row_id)

#2--Identify the rows the model uses
model_row_ids <- data_clean %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(proportion_primary_maximum_adj),               # response
    !is.na(n_paternities),                                 # weights
    !is.na(grp_composition_recode),                        # fixed effect
    !is.na(genus_species_subspecies),                      # phylo RE
    !is.na(species_nonphylo)                               # non‐phylo RE
  ) %>%
  pull(row_id)

#3--Which rows are in the table but not in the model?
rows_only_in_table <- setdiff(table_row_ids, model_row_ids)

#4--Which rows are in the model but not in the table?
rows_only_in_model <- setdiff(model_row_ids, table_row_ids)

#If both rows_only objects are empty, observations match

```

__Analysis 1: Description of relationship between paternity share and group composition__

We first evaluated the relationship between group composition and paternity share in the raw data (Table `r tbl_grptype_num`). On average, primary males obtained `r mean_val_primary_s`% (SD = `r sd_val_primary_s`%) of paternities across all group types; we obtained a similar value when all uncertain paternities were assigned to other males (mean = `r mean_val_primary_min_s`%, SD = `r sd_val_primary_min_s`%). 

For this analysis, we grouped the multi-male/single-female observations (n=`r n_mmf`) and multi-male/multi-female observations (n=`r n_mmff`) into a single multi-male category. These single-female groups could not be analyzed as a separate category due to the small sample size, and both observations had primary male paternity share proportions that were well within the range for multi-male/multi-female groups (Table `r tbl_grptype_num`, Figure `r fig_patloss`). 

Primary males in single-male/multi-female groups had the highest average paternity share (posterior mean=`r MFF_mean_prop_s`, 95% CI: `r MFF_lo_prop_s` - `r MFF_hi_prop_s`) and those in multi-male groups the lowest average share (posterior mean=`r MM_mean_prop_s`, 95% CI: `r MM_lo_prop_s` - `r MM_hi_prop_s`), though the variation within each category was considerable (Table `r tbl_grptype_num`, Figure `r fig_patloss`). The difference between single-male/multi-female and multi-male groups was credible (95% CI: `r MM_MFF_lo_prop_s` - `r MM_MFF_hi_prop_s`), while the contrast between pair-living and multi-male groups was weaker, with a 95% CI that barely crossed zero (`r MM_MF_lo_prop_s` - `r MM_MF_hi_prop_s`). The unexpectedly low paternity share in pair-living species was driven by dispersed pairs rather than cohesive pairs (Figure `r fig_patloss`).

```{r summary_table, echo=FALSE}

summary_table

```

```{r fig3-paternity-loss-model-plot, echo=FALSE, results='hide', fig.cap=fig_patloss_cap}

# Get conditional effects from the model
pl_ce <- conditional_effects(paternityloss_grptype_model, "grp_composition_recode")
pl_ce_data <- pl_ce$grp_composition_recode

# Plot conditional effects with raw data, colored by MF pairbond type

# 1. Create a new column to classify for color
data_clean <- data_clean %>%
  mutate(plot_color = case_when(
    grp_composition_recode == "MF" & pairbond_type == "cohesive" ~ "Cohesive MF",
    grp_composition_recode == "MF" & pairbond_type == "dispersed" ~ "Dispersed MF",
    grp_composition == "MMF" ~ "MMF",
    TRUE ~ "Other"
  ))

# 2. Compute raw means for the two MF subtypes (for diamond + label)
mf_means <- data_clean %>%
  filter(grp_composition_recode == "MF", !is.na(pairbond_type)) %>%
  group_by(grp_composition_recode, plot_color) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_val = mean(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(label = if_else(plot_color == "Cohesive MF", "cohesive", "dispersed"))

# 3. Define custom colors
mf_colors <- c(
  "Cohesive MF" = "#1b9e77",   # green
  "Dispersed MF" = "#d95f02",  # orange
  "MMF" = "#2196F3",           # blue
  "Other" = "gray60"
)

# 4. Compute mean position for MMF label
mmf_means <- data_clean %>%
  filter(plot_color == "MMF") %>%
  summarise(
    grp_composition_recode = first(grp_composition_recode),
    mean_val = mean(proportion_primary_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# 4. Plot
ggplot(pl_ce_data, aes(x = effect1__, y = estimate__)) +
  # Raw data points
  geom_jitter(data = data_clean,
              aes(x = grp_composition_recode, y = proportion_primary_maximum_adj, color = plot_color),
              width = 0.1, height = 0, alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +

  # Posterior means and CIs
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "black") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower__, ymax = upper__), width = 0.2, linewidth = 0.8) +

  # Colored diamonds for MF subtypes
  stat_summary(
    data = data_clean %>% filter(plot_color %in% c("Cohesive MF", "Dispersed MF")),
    aes(x = grp_composition_recode, y = proportion_primary_maximum_adj, color = plot_color),
    fun = mean,
    geom = "point",
    shape = 18,  # diamond
    size = 4
  ) +

  # Labels for each MF subtype mean (offset for visibility)
geom_text(
  data = mf_means,
  aes(x = grp_composition_recode, y = mean_val, label = label, color = plot_color),
  size = 4,
  fontface = "italic",
  hjust = 0,
  nudge_x = 0.15,
  nudge_y = -0.02
) +

  # Label for MMF observations
geom_text(
  data = mmf_means,
  aes(x = grp_composition_recode, y = mean_val, label = "multi-male/\nsingle-female"),
  color = "#2196F3",
  size = 4,
  fontface = "italic",
  hjust = 0,
  nudge_x = 0.12,
  nudge_y = -0.02
) +

  scale_color_manual(values = mf_colors) +
  guides(color = "none") +  # remove legend

  labs(
    x = "Group composition",
    y = "Estimated primary male paternity share",
    title = "Predicted paternity share by group type"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y = element_text(margin = margin(r = 10)),
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
  )




```

```{r competition-monopolizability-model, echo=FALSE, results='hide', fig.show='hide'}

if (file.exists("../output/model-objects/competition_monopolizability_model.rds")) {
  competition_monopolizability_model <- readRDS("../output/model-objects/competition_monopolizability_model.rds")
} else {
  competition_monopolizability_model <- brm(
    formula = bf(
      proportion_primary_maximum_adj | weights(n_paternities^0.5) ~ 
        n + m + m2 + n:m +
        (1 | gr(genus_species_subspecies, cov = phylo_matrix)) + 
        (1 | species_nonphylo)
    ),
    data = data_clean,
    family = Beta(link = "logit"),
    data2 = list(phylo_matrix = phylo_matrix),
    prior = c(
      prior(normal(0, 1), class = "Intercept"),
      prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"),
      prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "sd"),
      prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "phi")
    ),
    control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),
    chains = 4, cores = 4, iter = 10000, seed = 123
  )
  saveRDS(competition_monopolizability_model, "../output/model-objects/competition_monopolizability_model.rds")
}

summary(competition_monopolizability_model)
```

```{r extract-means-CIs-competition-monopolizability-model, echo=FALSE, results='hide', fig.show='hide'}

#####Extract means and CIs for competition var; how big is the diff between multi and single male groups?######

# Extract posterior draws
dr <- as_draws_df(competition_monopolizability_model)

# Compute linear predictors for SM and MM group type, holding m/m^2 constant
# Choose mean m (seasonality)
m_val <- mean(data_clean$m, na.rm = TRUE)
m2_val <- m_val^2

# Group 1: MM (n = 0) — reference group
eta_MM <- dr$b_Intercept + 
          dr$b_m   * m_val + 
          dr$b_m2  * m2_val

# Group 2: SM (n = 1)
eta_SM <- dr$b_Intercept + 
          dr$b_n   + 
          dr$b_m   * m_val + 
          dr$b_m2  * m2_val + 
          dr$`b_n:m` * m_val

# Transform to response scale (inverse logit)
mu_MM <- plogis(eta_MM)
mu_SM <- plogis(eta_SM)

# Summarize posterior for each group (reusing summ_vec from earlier)
g_MM <- summ_vec(mu_MM)
g_SM <- summ_vec(mu_SM)

# Format for use in text (reusing fmt2 from earlier)

MM_mean_prop_s <- fmt2(g_MM["mean"])
MM_lo_prop_s   <- fmt2(g_MM["lo"])
MM_hi_prop_s   <- fmt2(g_MM["hi"])

SM_mean_prop_s <- fmt2(g_SM["mean"])
SM_lo_prop_s   <- fmt2(g_SM["lo"])
SM_hi_prop_s   <- fmt2(g_SM["hi"])

# Calculate difference in group types across posterior draws
diff_SM_MM <- mu_SM - mu_MM

#Summary of the group differnce (mean/CI)
d_SM_MM <- summ_vec(diff_SM_MM)
d_SM_MM

#Format for use in text
SM_MM_diff_prop_s  <- fmt2(d_SM_MM["mean"])
SM_MM_lo_prop_s    <- fmt2(d_SM_MM["lo"])
SM_MM_hi_prop_s    <- fmt2(d_SM_MM["hi"])

# Sample size for this model (smaller than Analysis 1 b/c not all species have seasonality data)
n_obs_cm <- nobs(competition_monopolizability_model)
n_species_cm <- length(unique(
  sub("^([^_]+_[^_]+).*", "\\1", competition_monopolizability_model$data$genus_species_subspecies)
))
n_pats_cm <- sum(competition_monopolizability_model$data$n_paternities, na.rm = TRUE)

n_obs_cm_fmt <- format(n_obs_cm, big.mark = ",")
n_species_cm_fmt <- format(n_species_cm, big.mark = ",")
n_pats_cm_fmt <- format(n_pats_cm, big.mark = ",")

```

```{r plot-competition-monopolizability-model, echo=FALSE, results='hide', fig.show='hide'}

#################Plot competition monopolizability model#############

# Choose m levels 
m_vals <- quantile(data_clean$m, probs = c(0.1, 0.5, 0.9), na.rm = TRUE)

newdat_B <- expand.grid(
  n = c(0, 1),
  m = as.numeric(m_vals)
) %>%
  mutate(
    m2    = m^2,
    n_lab = factor(n, levels = c(0,1), labels = c("MM", "SM")),
    m_lab = paste0("m = ", round(m, 2))
  )

# Get summaries and rename to mean/lo/hi
pred_B <- fitted(
  competition_monopolizability_model,
  newdata    = newdat_B,
  re_formula = NA,
  summary    = TRUE,
  scale      = "response"
) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  transmute(mean = Estimate, lo = `Q2.5`, hi = `Q97.5`)

summ_B <- bind_cols(newdat_B, pred_B)

# Sanity check (should include mean, lo, hi)
# print(names(summ_B))

# Order legend by increasing m
levels_m <- paste0("m = ", round(sort(unique(summ_B$m)), 2))
summ_B <- summ_B %>%
  mutate(m_lab = factor(m_lab, levels = levels_m))

# Color-blind friendly palette for figure
okabe_ito <- c("#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#E69F00", "#0072B2",
               "#D55E00", "#CC79A7", "#F0E442", "#000000")

pd <- position_dodge(width = 0.35)
brks <- levels(summ_B$m_lab)

cm_plot <- ggplot(summ_B, aes(x = n_lab, y = mean, ymin = lo, ymax = hi, color = m_lab)) +
  geom_point(position = pd, size = 2.8, shape = 16) +   
  geom_errorbar(position = pd, width = 0.12, linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = okabe_ito, name = "Monopolizability (m)") +
  labs(x = "Group composition", y = "Estimated primary male paternity share") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "right") +
  scale_color_manual(
    breaks = brks,
    labels = c("most seasonal", "moderately seasonal", "least seasonal"),
    values = okabe_ito[seq_along(brks)],  
    name   = "Seasonality")

```

```{r derivatives-competition-monopolizability-model, echo=FALSE, results='hide', fig.show='hide'}

######Extract derivatives; as monopolizability increases, how fast does paternity share change?

# Partial derivative of logit(mu) w/r/t monopolizability at the mean
m_deriv <- mean(data_clean$m, na.rm = TRUE)

# Logit-scale derivative: d logit(mu) / dm = b_m + 2*b_m2*m + b_n:m * n
dlogit_MM <- dr$b_m + 2 * dr$b_m2 * m_deriv                    # n = 0
dlogit_SM <- dr$b_m + 2 * dr$b_m2 * m_deriv + dr$`b_n:m`       # n = 1

# Response-scale derivative via chain rule: d mu / dm = dlogit/dm * mu * (1 - mu)
# Reuse eta and mu from earlier chunk (eta_MM, eta_SM, mu_MM, mu_SM)
dresp_MM <- dlogit_MM * mu_MM * (1 - mu_MM)
dresp_SM <- dlogit_SM * mu_SM * (1 - mu_SM)

# Summarize
summ_vec(dlogit_MM)
summ_vec(dlogit_SM)
summ_vec(dresp_MM)
summ_vec(dresp_SM)

# Format for text
dresp_MM_mean <- fmt2(summ_vec(dresp_MM)["mean"])
dresp_MM_lo   <- fmt2(summ_vec(dresp_MM)["lo"])
dresp_MM_hi   <- fmt2(summ_vec(dresp_MM)["hi"])

dresp_SM_mean <- fmt2(summ_vec(dresp_SM)["mean"])
dresp_SM_lo   <- fmt2(summ_vec(dresp_SM)["lo"])
dresp_SM_hi   <- fmt2(summ_vec(dresp_SM)["hi"])

```

__Analysis 2: Evaluation of the presence of in-group competitors and seasonality__

We next examined how the presence of both in-group competitors and seasonality influenced the paternity share of primary males. Seasonality data were not available for all species, so this analysis was based on a slightly smaller sample than Analysis 1 (`r n_obs_cm_fmt` observations, `r n_species_cm_fmt` species, and `r n_pats_cm_fmt` total paternities). In these models, both types of single-male groups (i.e., pair-living and single-male/multi-female) were combined into one category called single-male, because Analysis 1 estimates of primary males’ paternity shares in these groups were not reliably different and because they share the same key characteristic of having no in-group competitors. 

The extent of reproductive seasonality had little effect on primary male paternity share, regardless of whether males lived in single or multi-male groups (Figure `r fig_seasonality`). Consistent with this overall conclusion, the estimated change in paternity share per unit increase in monopolizability was near zero in both multi-male groups (posterior mean = `r dresp_MM_mean`, 95% CI: `r dresp_MM_lo` - `r dresp_MM_hi`) and in single-male groups (posterior mean = `r dresp_SM_mean`, 95% CI: `r dresp_SM_lo` - `r dresp_SM_hi`). The results were qualitatively very similar when pair-living groups, where mating opportunities should be highly monopolizable regardless of seasonality, were excluded. 

Adding seasonality to the model slightly lowered the expected paternity share for primary males (Figure `r fig_seasonality`). Here, for multi-male groups the posterior mean was `r MM_mean_prop_s`, while for single-male groups it was `r SM_mean_prop_s`. The 95% CI of the difference between the two did not include zero (95% CI: `r SM_MM_lo_prop_s` - `r SM_MM_hi_prop_s`), indicating a credible difference. 

```{r competition-monopolizability-plot, echo=FALSE, fig.cap=fig_seasonality_cap}

cm_plot

```

**Question 3: When primary males in multimale groups lose paternity, who are they losing it to?**

```{r egp-proportions-nums, echo=FALSE, results='hide'}

# Raw summary numbers — need this in order to fill in inline text numbers plus table

# A single “raw” summary with full precision
summary_data_egps_raw <- data_clean %>%
  filter(grp_composition_recode == "MM") %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(proportion_egp_minimum_adj),
    !is.na(proportion_egp_maximum_adj)
  ) %>%
  summarise(
    n_obs     = n(),                                            # obs
    n_pats    = sum(n_paternities, na.rm = TRUE),               # total paternities
    n_species = n_distinct(species_10ktrees),           # species
    min       = min(proportion_egp_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE), # raw min prop
    max       = max(proportion_egp_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE), # raw max prop
    mean_min  = mean(proportion_egp_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE), # raw mean(min)
    sd_min    = sd(proportion_egp_minimum_adj, na.rm = TRUE),   # raw sd(min)
    mean_max  = mean(proportion_egp_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE), # raw mean(max)
    sd_max    = sd(proportion_egp_maximum_adj, na.rm = TRUE)    # raw sd(max)
  )

# Reformatting numbers for use in text
mean_min_str <- sprintf("%.0f", summary_data_egps_raw$mean_min * 100)
sd_min_str   <- sprintf("%.0f", summary_data_egps_raw$sd_min   * 100)
mean_max_str <- sprintf("%.0f", summary_data_egps_raw$mean_max * 100)
sd_max_str   <- sprintf("%.0f", summary_data_egps_raw$sd_max   * 100)
obs_str      <- format(summary_data_egps_raw$n_obs,     big.mark = ",")
species_str  <- format(summary_data_egps_raw$n_species, big.mark = ",")
pats_str     <- format(summary_data_egps_raw$n_pats,    big.mark = ",")

# EGP share of "lost" paternities (using Analysis 1 MM posterior mean)
egp_loss_share_min <- summary_data_egps_raw$mean_min / (1 - MM_mean_prop_A1)
egp_loss_share_max <- summary_data_egps_raw$mean_max / (1 - MM_mean_prop_A1)
egp_loss_share_min_str <- sprintf("%.0f", egp_loss_share_min * 100)
egp_loss_share_max_str <- sprintf("%.0f", egp_loss_share_max * 100)

```





```{r troubleshooting-n-differences, echo=FALSE, results='hide', eval=FALSE}

#Figure out why I get more EGP values than primary male values for MMFF groups.

# Count MMF and MMFF rows with different missingness profiles
data_clean %>%
  filter(grp_composition_recode == "MM") %>%
  mutate(
    has_primary = !is.na(proportion_primary_minimum_adj) & !is.na(proportion_primary_maximum_adj),
    has_egp = !is.na(proportion_egp_minimum_adj) & !is.na(proportion_egp_maximum_adj)
  ) %>%
  count(grp_composition, has_primary, has_egp)

# Identify rows where EGP data exist but primary paternity data do not
egp_only_cases <- data_clean %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(proportion_egp_minimum_adj) & !is.na(proportion_egp_maximum_adj) & 
    (is.na(proportion_primary_minimum_adj) | is.na(proportion_primary_maximum_adj))
  )

# Select relevant identifying information
egp_only_cases_summary <- egp_only_cases %>%
  select(genus_species_subspecies, reference, grp_composition) %>%
  distinct()

egp_only_cases_summary

```

```{r paternityloss-source-model, echo=FALSE, results='hide', fig.show='hide'}

loss_source_data_mm <- data_subset_mm %>%
  mutate(
    # Calculate in-group losses and reformat negatives
    n_igp        = as.integer(n_paternities - n_alphaprimary - n_egp),
    n_igp        = pmax(n_igp, 0L),
    # Total losses
    total_losses = n_egp + n_igp,
    # Prepare weights
    model_weights = n_paternities^0.5
  ) %>%
  filter(
    # Keep zero-loss cases (total_losses >= 0)
    !is.na(n_egp),
    !is.na(n_igp),
    !is.na(n_paternities),
    !is.na(genus_species_subspecies),
    !is.na(species_nonphylo)
  )


if (file.exists("../output/model-objects/loss_source_model_mm.rds")) {
  loss_source_model_mm <- readRDS("../output/model-objects/loss_source_model_mm.rds")
} else {
  loss_source_model_mm <- brm(
  bf(n_egp | trials(total_losses) ~
       (1 | gr(genus_species_subspecies, cov = phylo_matrix)) +
       (1 | species_nonphylo)),
  family = binomial(),
  data = loss_source_data_mm,
  data2 = list(phylo_matrix = phylo_matrix),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(student_t(3, 0, 2.5), class = "sd")
  ),
  sample_prior = "no",
  chains = 4, iter = 10000, seed = 123,
  control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99)
)
  saveRDS(loss_source_model_mm, "../output/model-objects/loss_source_model_mm.rds")
}

summary(loss_source_model_mm)

# Use posterior_linpred (not posterior_epred) to get the probability (pi)
# on the response scale. posterior_epred returns expected counts for binomial
# models (n * pi), which is not what we want here.
egp_loss_summary <- posterior_linpred(loss_source_model_mm, transform = TRUE, re_formula = NA) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  summarise(
    mean = round(mean(V1) * 100, 0),
    ci_lower = round(quantile(V1, 0.025) * 100, 0),
    ci_upper = round(quantile(V1, 0.975) * 100, 0)
  )

egp_loss_summary

igp_loss_summary <- posterior_linpred(loss_source_model_mm, transform = TRUE, re_formula = NA) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  summarise(
    mean = round(mean(1 - V1) * 100, 0),
    ci_lower = round(quantile(1 - V1, 0.025) * 100, 0),
    ci_upper = round(quantile(1 - V1, 0.975) * 100, 0)
  )

igp_loss_summary

n_obs_loss_source <- nrow(loss_source_model_mm$data)
n_species_loss_source <- length(unique(
  sub("^([^_]+_[^_]+).*", "\\1", loss_source_model_mm$data$genus_species_subspecies)
))
n_pats_loss_source <- sum(loss_source_data_mm$n_paternities, na.rm = TRUE)

n_obs_loss_source_fmt <- format(n_obs_loss_source, big.mark = ",")
n_species_loss_source_fmt <- format(n_species_loss_source, big.mark = ",")
n_pats_loss_source_fmt <- format(n_pats_loss_source, big.mark = ",")

# Derived quantities for inline text: overall EGP rate based on Analysis 1 CI and model EGP share
loss_hi_A1 <- 1 - MM_lo_prop_A1   # max share lost (from low end of CI)
loss_lo_A1 <- 1 - MM_hi_prop_A1   # min share lost (from high end of CI)
loss_lo_pct_s <- sprintf("%.0f", loss_lo_A1 * 100)
loss_hi_pct_s <- sprintf("%.0f", loss_hi_A1 * 100)

egp_frac <- egp_loss_summary$mean / 100   # model-estimated EGP proportion of losses
overall_egp_lo <- egp_frac * loss_lo_A1
overall_egp_hi <- egp_frac * loss_hi_A1
overall_egp_lo_s <- sprintf("%.0f", overall_egp_lo * 100)
overall_egp_hi_s <- sprintf("%.0f", overall_egp_hi * 100)

pp_check(loss_source_model_mm, type = "hist")
pp_check(loss_source_model_mm, type = "dens_overlay")

```

```{r loss-source-dropped-obs-diagnostic, echo=FALSE, results='hide', eval=FALSE}

#Determine why a bunch of obs drop from the model

#1 Rows used in the raw summary
raw_ids <- data_clean %>%
  filter(
    grp_composition_recode == "MM",
    !is.na(proportion_egp_minimum_adj),
    !is.na(proportion_egp_maximum_adj)
  ) %>%
  pull(row_id)

#2 Rows used in the data for model 
model_ids <- loss_source_data_mm$row_id

#3 Which rows are in the raw summary but not in the model?
dropped_ids <- setdiff(raw_ids, model_ids)

#4 Look at the dropped rows
dropped_obs <- data_clean %>%
  filter(row_id %in% dropped_ids) %>%
  select(
    row_id,
    grp_composition_recode,
    proportion_egp_minimum_adj,
    proportion_egp_maximum_adj,
    n_paternities,
    n_egp,
    n_alphaprimary,
    genus_species_subspecies,
    species_nonphylo
  )

#5 Print them all so I can see exactly why each was dropped // These are rows that do not have n_primaryalpha, which is necessary to calculate losses.

#print(dropped_obs, n = Inf)

```

In the raw data, a minimum of `r mean_min_str`% (SD = `r sd_min_str`%) of paternities in multi-male groups were assigned to extra-group males; this rose to `r mean_max_str`% (SD = `r sd_max_str`%) if all uncertain paternities were assigned to extra‐group males (n=`r obs_str` observations, `r species_str` species, and `r pats_str` total paternities). If primary males in multi-male groups sire approximately `r MM_mean_pct_A1_s`% of infants (i.e., the posterior mean prediction from Question 2, Analysis 1 above), then about `r sprintf("%.0f", (1 - MM_mean_prop_A1) * 100)`% of infants are sired by other males. Given a raw, overall EGP rate of `r mean_min_str`–`r mean_max_str`%, this would mean that `r egp_loss_share_min_str`–`r egp_loss_share_max_str`% of the paternities that primary males lose are attributable to extra-group males, with the remainder lost to group mates.^[`r mean_min_str`/`r sprintf("%.0f", (1 - MM_mean_prop_A1) * 100)` = `r egp_loss_share_min_str`%; `r mean_max_str`/`r sprintf("%.0f", (1 - MM_mean_prop_A1) * 100)` = `r egp_loss_share_max_str`%.]

While our statistical models generally tell a similar story, they suggest that losses to extra-group males may be slightly higher than values obtained from the raw data. For the subset of data where all relevant information was available (`r n_obs_loss_source_fmt` observations, `r n_species_loss_source_fmt` species, and `r n_pats_loss_source_fmt` paternities), an estimated `r egp_loss_summary$mean`% of primary males' lost paternities were EGPs (95% CI: `r egp_loss_summary$ci_lower` - `r egp_loss_summary$ci_upper`%). The other `r igp_loss_summary$mean`% of lost paternities went to in-group males (95% CI: `r igp_loss_summary$ci_lower` - `r igp_loss_summary$ci_upper`%). 

Since the 95% CI for primary male paternity share in multimale groups is `r MM_lo_pct_A1_s`-`r MM_hi_pct_A1_s`% (see Figure `r fig_patloss`), this means that somewhere between `r loss_lo_pct_s`% and `r loss_hi_pct_s`% of infants are sired by other males. If `r egp_loss_summary$mean`% of the low end and high end of these paternity share estimates are EGPs, this would translate to an overall EGP rate of ~`r overall_egp_lo_s`-`r overall_egp_hi_s`%.^[`r sprintf("%.2f", egp_frac)` × `r sprintf("%.2f", loss_lo_A1)` = `r sprintf("%.2f", overall_egp_lo)`; `r sprintf("%.2f", egp_frac)` × `r sprintf("%.2f", loss_hi_A1)` = `r sprintf("%.2f", overall_egp_hi)`.]    

## Discussion

One of the primary goals of this project was to compile all available genetic paternity data for wild nonhuman primates and produce a single publicly accessible repository for them. The database that we have created contains information about `r n_species_total` species from `r n_genera` genera representing `r n_families` of 14 primate families in 10kTrees. The three families that are not included (*Galagidae*, *Lorisidae*, and *Daubentonidae*) are all largely solitary Strepsirrhines. Although these numbers are gratifyingly large, we are missing information from a substantial number of species, while some families, particularly the *Cercopithecidae* and *Hominidae*, are over-represented in the dataset. 

Phylogeny has a detectable but moderate effect on the distribution of paternity in primate groups. Species-level random effects, which do not differentiate between phylogenetic and non-phylogenetic species differences, explained roughly 35–40% of the total variation in primary males’ paternity share. Our models indicate that between `r round(summary_lambda_iom_min[2] * 100)`% and `r round(summary_lambda_iom[2] * 100)`% of that inter-species variation is attributable to phylogenetic relationships. In absolute terms, this means phylogeny accounts for a relatively modest share of total variation in paternity outcomes. Instead, most of the variation came from observation-level differences within and between species that are not captured by the phylogenetic or species-level random effects. @KamilarCooper2013 found a strong phylogenetic signal associated with morphological traits related to the intensity of intrasexual selection on males, including sexual dimorphism in body mass ($\lambda$ = 0.94), canine size ($\lambda$ = 0.82), and testes size ($\lambda$ = 0.89). They also reported a Pagel’s $\lambda$ of 0.60 for the number of males in groups. Because our $\lambda$ is computed differently than Pagel’s $\lambda$ (i.e., on the random effects derived from our statistical model, rather than directly from the phylogenetic tree), we can't directly compare our estimates to those values. However, the general pattern is consistent: morphological traits associated with male-male competition show very strong phylogenetic signal, while behavioral and demographic traits like group composition and paternity share show comparatively weaker signals.

### Primary males’ paternity share is strongly influenced by the kinds of groups in which they live

Interestingly, pair-living non-human primate species encompass both lowest and highest rates of paternity loss for primary males. For species that live in cohesive pairs, primary males sire about 90% of their partners’ offspring. For species that live in dispersed pairs, primary males sire only about 55%. Similar findings have been reported for a broader range of pair-living mammalian species [@Clutton-BrockIsvaran2006; @Hucketal2014]. @CohasAllaine2009 found no difference between pair-living species that form dispersed and cohesive pair bonds, but they reported that solitary species have much higher rates of extra group paternities than pair-living species. However, they categorized as solitary at least one species, the fork-marked lemur (*Phaner furcifer*), that other researchers have described as having dispersed pair bonds [@SchulkeKappeler2003]. Thus, the preponderance of evidence suggests that for pair-living mammals, paternity patterns are strongly influenced by patterns of association. This highlights the problems with assuming that pair-living (or social monogamy) is a homogenous phenomena, a point which has been made before by other authors [e.g., @Fernandez-Duqueetal2020].

Males in single-male/multi-female groups sire the great majority of offspring within their groups, but do not monopolize paternity as successfully as males in cohesive pairs do. This is likely to reflect the fact that competition over access to groups of females is more intense than competition over access to single females and it is harder for males to monopolize multiple females than to monopolize single females. Blue monkeys, for example, typically form single-male/multi-female groups, but there are regular incursions by outside males during the mating season. Larger numbers of sexually receptive females draw larger numbers of outside males [@GaoCords2020]. 

Primary males living in multi-male groups lose more paternities than primary males living in cohesive pairs or in single-male/multi-female groups. @Clutton-BrockIsvaran2006 reported similar patterns. Paternity losses by primary males in multi-male groups may be the product of several different mechanisms. First, primary males may not be able to monopolize paternity when more than one sexually receptive female is present [@Altmann1962]. Overlap in female receptivity is particularly likely to occur in multi-male/multi-female groups because there are larger numbers of females in these groups [@Ostneretal2008]. Second, in species with long male tenures, females may avoid mating with closely related males. The tenures of primary males in white faced capuchin groups are sometimes long enough that females live in the same groups as their fathers, and males don’t sire the offspring of closely related females [@Godoyetal2016; @Wikbergetal2017]. Third, males in multi-male groups may form coalitions that influence their access to sexually receptive females. Middle-ranking male olive and yellow baboons team up to disrupt consortships of high-ranking males [@Packer1977; @Noe1992], although we don’t know whether these consort takeovers influence paternity. Male chimpanzees rely on their social ties with other males to obtain and maintain high rank and to defend the boundaries of their territories. Alpha males selectively tolerate matings by their allies [@Brayetal2016; @Duffyetal2007], and this translates into paternity success for males with close ties to the alpha male [@Feldblumetal2021].

### Primary males in multi-male groups mostly lose paternities to other resident males 

Our data also show that primary males in multi-male groups lose the majority of paternities to other males within their groups, not to extra-group males. This may be because resident males actively deter incursions by outside males or because subordinate residents are better able to capitalize on reproductive opportunities than outside males. For example, male chacma baboons “monitor consortships so assiduously that they rapidly recognize both when an unexpected mating opportunity arises and when a consortship has ended“ [@Crockfordetal2007, p. 889]. This may give them an important advantage over extra-group males. 

### Seasonality has little effect on primary males' paternity share

Contrary to our predictions, the extent of reproductive seasonality had little effect on the paternity share of primary males.  This prediction was based on the assumption that when reproductive seasonality was strong, there would be more extensive overlap in females’ sexual receptivity, and this would limit primary males’ ability to monopolize conceptions.  In retrospect, we should have been more skeptical of this assumption. @GogartenKoenig2013 showed that male reproductive skew declines as the extent of reproductive synchrony among females increases in non-human primate groups, but the extent of reproductive synchrony among females does not vary consistently with the extent of reproductive seasonality. 


### Limitations of the study

Our study has a number of limitations. First, almost all of the samples are incomplete. In some cases, not all infants born in the population during the study period were sampled, not all potential sires were sampled, or it was not possible to exclude more than one male as the sire of a particular infant. We have estimated the minimum and maximum values of the primary males’ paternity share and the proportion of extra-group paternities, but the real values lie somewhere in between. Second, most species are represented by a single study population, and sometimes a single group. This may bias estimates of the range of variation within and between species (see Alberts et al. XXXX). Third, many species are missing from our database and certain taxa are over-represented.  

In addition, the exercise of writing out our theoretical model made it clear that there is a significant disconnect between the data that would ideally be available to answer our questions, versus the data that are actually available. In particular, it would be best to use conception-level data, to have quantitative information about the degree of extra-group competition that males face, and to have counts of the number of other sexually receptive females present during the probable window of conception. We acknowledge that these are challenging data to collect, especially in species with concealed ovulation. 

### Conclusions

In many respects, our analysis of the largest genetic paternity set assembled on primates to date confirms results from other studies. Primary males consistently obtain the largest share of paternities, but their odds are lower when they live in groups with other males than when they do not. It is also clear that despite this general pattern, there is considerable variation in primary males' ability to control reproduction in their groups, regardless of social group structure. 

However, our results also contained some surprises. Breeding seasonality, which in theory should be a proxy for males' ability to monopolize mating opportunities, did not predict primary males' paternity share regardless of the type of group they lived in (though note that prior studies have found mixed evidence both for effects of seasonality itself, and for the idea that seasonality necessarily means high reproductive synchrony: @KutsukakeNunn2006; @Ostneretal2008; @Paul1997; @GogartenKoenig2013). In addition, pair-living species had both the highest and lowest rates of primary male paternity share. We hope that the availability of our living database will help other researchers 

# Acknowledgements

We would like to thank Adrian Jaggi, Eduardo Fernandez-Duque, and Jason Kamilar for their advice and thoughts on these analyses, as well as Stephanie Fox and Pascale Sicotte, Carola Borries, Sylvain Gatti, Liza Moscovice and Robert Seyfarth, and [XXXX whoever else Joan wants to thank for the olive baboon data] for contributing unpublished paternity. Maud Mouginot, Amy Scott, Joseph Feldblum, Songtao Guo, Caroline Nievergelt, Carola Borries, and [who am I forgetting? XXXX]  all helped us untangle and extract data from published papers, for which we are extremely grateful. 

# References
